title: César E. Chávez and the Farmworker Movement National Historical Park Act
short_title: César E. Chávez and the Farmworker Movement National Historical Park Act
plain_summary: This law changes the name of the César E. Chávez National Monument to the César E. Chávez and the Farmworker Movement National Historical Park. It also lets the park grow bigger by adding more important places in California and Arizona that are connected to César Chávez and farmworkers. The law tells the government to study the path that farmworkers walked in 1966 when they marched from Delano to Sacramento, California. The study will help decide if this march route should become a national historic trail.
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Natural Resources.

sections.title: Section 1 Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the César E. Chávez and the Farmworker Movement National Historical Park Act.

sections.title: Section 2 Findings and Purpose
sections.text: Congress has found several important facts. On October eighth, two thousand twelve, the César E. Chávez National Monument was created by a presidential order. This monument protects important places connected to a property in Keene, California, called Nuestra Señora Reina de la Paz. This place served as the main headquarters of the United Farm Workers union. It was also the home and workplace of César E. Chávez, his family, union members, and his supporters. While this monument honors the amazing work and contributions that César Chávez and the farmworker movement made to United States history, there are other important places in California and Arizona that are also part of this story. The National Park Service did a study called César Chavez Special Resource Study and Environmental Assessment that was given to Congress on October twenty-fourth, two thousand thirteen. In this study, the Park Service found that several places connected to César E. Chávez and the farmworker movement would be good additions to the National Park System. These places show a special and important part of United States history that is not properly shown at existing national parks. The Park Service recommended that Congress create a national historical park to honor the role that César E. Chávez played in improving the lives of farmworkers. The study also found that the route of the nineteen sixty-six march from Delano to Sacramento, California, meets the standards to become a National Historic Landmark. The Park Service recommended that this route should be studied more to see if it could become a national historic trail. They also said the Park Service could work with partner groups and agencies to create programs that explain the history of this nineteen sixty-six march route. The purpose of this law is to create the César E. Chávez and the Farmworker Movement National Historical Park. This park will help preserve, protect, and explain the nationally important places connected to César Chávez and the farmworker movement. It will help people understand the extraordinary achievements and contributions to United States history made by César Chávez and the farmworker movement. It will also support and strengthen the network of places and resources connected to César Chávez and the farmworker movement.

sections.title: Section 3 Definitions
sections.text: In this law, these terms mean the following things. Historical park means the César E. Chávez and the Farmworker Movement National Historical Park that is created by section four of this law. Map means the map called César E. Chávez and the Farmworker Movement National Historical Park Proposed Boundary, numbered five zero two slash one seven nine eight five seven B, and dated September two thousand twenty-two. Secretary means the Secretary of the Interior. States means the State of California and the State of Arizona. Study means the study done by the National Park Service called César Chavez Special Resource Study and Environmental Assessment that was given to Congress on October twenty-fourth, two thousand thirteen.

sections.title: Section 4 César E. Chávez and the Farmworker Movement National Historical Park
sections.text: The César E. Chávez National Monument that was created on October eighth, two thousand twelve, by Presidential Order number eight eight eight four is now renamed as the César E. Chávez and the Farmworker Movement National Historical Park. Any money that was available for the old monument can now be used for the historical park. Any mention in laws, rules, documents, records, maps, or other official United States papers of the old monument will now be considered a mention of the César E. Chávez and the Farmworker Movement National Historical Park. The boundary of the historical park will include the area shown as César E. Chávez National Monument in Keene, California, as shown on the Map. The Secretary may also include these additional places within the park boundary as shown on the Map: The Forty Acres in Delano, California; Santa Rita Center in Phoenix, Arizona; and McDonnell Hall in San Jose, California. However, these additional places cannot be included in the park until the Secretary either buys the land or gets rights to use the land, or makes a written agreement with the owner that says the place will be managed according to this law. Within thirty days after the Secretary adds one of these places to the historical park, the Secretary must publish a notice in the Federal Register announcing the addition. The Map will be available for the public to look at in the proper National Park Service offices. The Secretary may buy land and rights to land within the area shown on the Map as Proposed NPS Boundary through gifts, purchases from willing sellers using donated or government money, or trades. The Secretary will manage the historical park according to this section and the laws that generally apply to all units of the National Park System. The Secretary may provide technical help and public education about historic places, museums, and resources on land not managed by the Secretary that relate to the life of César E. Chávez and the history of the farmworker movement. The Secretary may make cooperative agreements with the States, local governments, public and private organizations, and individuals to help preserve, develop, interpret, and use the historical park. Within three years after money is made available to do this work, the Secretary must prepare a general management plan for the historical park following the rules in federal law. This management plan must include a decision about whether there are places in the Coachella Valley in California that were reviewed in the Study that should be added to the historical park, additional representative places in the States that were reviewed in the Study that should be added to the historical park, or places outside of the States in the United States that relate to the farmworker movement that should be connected to and interpreted at the historical park. When the management plan is finished, the Secretary must give any recommendations for additional places to be included in the historical park to the Committee on Energy and Natural Resources of the Senate and the Committee on Natural Resources of the House of Representatives. The management plan must be prepared after talking with any owner of land that is included within the boundaries of the historical park and appropriate Federal, State, and Tribal agencies, public and private organizations, and individuals, including the National Chavez Center and the César Chávez Foundation.

sections.title: Section 5 Farmworker Peregrinacion National Historical Trail Study
sections.text: The National Trails System Act is changed by adding the following new trail to the list: Farmworker peregrinacion national historic trail. This is the Farmworker Peregrinacion National Historic Trail, a route of about three hundred miles taken by farmworkers between Delano and Sacramento, California, in nineteen sixty-six, as generally shown as Alternative C in the study done by the National Park Service called César Chavez Special Resource Study and Environmental Assessment that was given to Congress on October twenty-fourth, two thousand thirteen.
